era_context: The early 11th century witnessed the fragmentation of the Caliphate of Córdoba in al-Andalus, the consolidation of the Capetian dynasty in France under Robert II, the expansion of the Ziyarid and Kakuyid dynasties in Persia, and the reign of Cnut II in England and Denmark. In East Asia, the Northern Song dynasty was consolidating control over China, while the Khmer Empire under Suryavarman I was expanding in Southeast Asia. This was an era of regional consolidation, religious reform movements, and the early development of feudal structures in Western Europe.
geographic_coverage_gaps: Sub-Saharan Africa (limited written sources; archaeological record underexplored)

events.title: Death of Robert II of France; accession of Henry I
events.description: Robert II, known as Robert the Pious, died on 20 July 1034 after a 48-year reign. He was succeeded by his son Henry I, who faced immediate challenges to his authority from rebellious nobles and his own mother, Constance of Arles, who initially acted as regent. Henry I's early reign was marked by struggles to consolidate royal power against feudal fragmentation. This succession marked a critical moment in the development of Capetian authority in France.

sources.name: Raoul Glaber, Historiae (Five Books of Histories)

events.certainty_note: Death date of Robert II is well-attested in contemporary chronicles. Succession is documented in multiple near-contemporary sources.

events.title: Anund Jacob of Sweden defeats Stenkil in succession dispute
events.description: Following the death of Olof Skötkonung around 1022, succession disputes in Sweden continued. Anund Jacob, son of Olof, consolidated power over rival claimants including Stenkil. This period saw the gradual Christianization of Sweden and the emergence of a more centralized kingdom. The exact chronology of Swedish succession in this period is uncertain, as contemporary documentation is sparse.

sources.name: Adam of Bremen, Gesta Hammaburgensis Ecclesiae Pontificum

events.certainty_note: Swedish succession chronology in this period is poorly documented. Adam of Bremen wrote in the late 11th century. Dating is approximate within ±5–10 years.

events.title: Suryavarman I consolidates Khmer Empire control
events.description: Suryavarman I (r. 1002–1050) continued his expansion and consolidation of the Khmer Empire, extending control over neighboring territories and strengthening administrative structures. By 1034, he had established a powerful centralized state with sophisticated irrigation systems and monumental architecture. His reign marked the height of early Khmer power before the later construction of Angkor Wat under Suryavarman II.
events.certainty_note: Suryavarman I's reign dates are established through epigraphic evidence and Chinese records. Specific events within 1034 are less precisely dated.

events.title: Caliph al-Qa'im continues consolidation in Baghdad
events.description: Al-Qa'im bi-Amr Allah (r. 1031–1075) was in the early years of his long caliphate, during which he worked to restore Abbasid authority in Iraq and Baghdad against Buyid and Seljuk pressure. In 1034, the caliphate remained fragmented, with real power held by military emirs and regional dynasties. Al-Qa'im's reign would eventually see the rise of Seljuk influence in the region.

sources.name: Ibn al-Athir, Al-Kamil fi al-Ta'rikh (The Complete History)
Miskawayh, Tajārib al-umam (Experiences of the Nations)

events.certainty_note: Al-Qa'im's reign dates are well-established. Specific events of 1034 are less precisely documented in surviving sources.

events.title: Ziyarid dynasty consolidates power in Tabaristan
events.description: The Ziyarid dynasty, ruling the region south of the Caspian Sea, continued to consolidate power in the early 11th century. By 1034, the Ziyarids maintained a relatively stable state in Tabaristan, though they faced pressure from Kakuyid and Seljuk expansion. The dynasty would eventually be absorbed by Seljuk expansion in the mid-11th century.
events.certainty_note: Ziyarid history in this period is poorly documented in surviving sources. Numismatic evidence provides some confirmation of dynastic continuity.

disconfirming_evidence: The exact chronology of Swedish succession in the early 11th century is disputed. Some sources place Stenkil's rise to power earlier or later than 1034. The significance of specific events in 1034 for the Ziyarid and Kakuyid dynasties is difficult to establish from surviving sources, and the traditional narrative of their consolidation may overstate the degree of centralization achieved. No major disconfirming evidence identified for the French succession or Khmer/Abbasid developments.
historiographic_note: Documentation for 1034 is uneven. Western European sources (particularly French and English chronicles) are relatively rich, though often written decades after events. Islamic sources are abundant but frequently compiled centuries later (Ibn al-Athir wrote in the 13th century). East Asian sources (Chinese records) are contemporary and detailed. Scandinavian and Central Asian documentation is sparse. The surviving record is heavily weighted toward political and military events; economic, social, and cultural developments are underrepresented. Regional biases favor the Mediterranean, Near East, and East Asia over sub-Saharan Africa, the Americas, and Oceania, where written sources are minimal or absent.

graph_edges.note: Henry I's accession in 1034 initiated a 26-year reign that shaped French feudalism
Suryavarman I's consolidation laid groundwork for later Khmer expansion under Suryavarman II
